content.text:
High-Level Design: Design A Chat Application like Facebook Messenger, WhatsApp, Slack, Discord, etc.
MEDIUM
role
Software Development Engineer
Round 1
System Design Round
System Design (HLD)
exp
#833 · Nov 2025
○ no offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
System Design
Backend
High Availability
High Scalability
Low Latency
Microservices

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Required features (Functional Requirements):
It should support one-on-one conversations between users.
It should support group conversations between users.
User should recieve push notifications about any new messages if they are not online.
Other requirements which can be asked:
It should support one-on-one conversations between users. (media -audio, video and text)
It should keep track of the online/offline statuses of its users.
It should support the persistent and durable storage of chat history.
It should support read receipts
Support sending attachments like image, videos etc.
User status - showing Online| Offline| Away| Private
Not functional requirements:
System should be highly consistent
There should be minimum latency while chatting
Scalability
End-to-end encryption
Global system

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — message delivery, online presence, and group fan-out — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in a chat system: **a message must feel instant (sub-100ms delivery) yet never be lost** — so we're balancing low-latency real-time push against durable, ordered, exactly-once-feeling persistence.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/WhatsApp-style bar** (massive scale, read/delivery hot-path obsession). Tell me if it's a Slack/Discord-style workspace product and I'll re-tilt toward channels and rich presence.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Users should be able to send & receive 1:1 messages in real time** (the heart of it).
- **Users should be able to send & receive group messages.**
- **Users should receive push notifications for new messages when offline.**

**Below the line (out of scope for the main pass, will mention):**
- Read receipts / delivery receipts (I'll touch in deep dives).
- Online/offline/away presence (I'll design it as a deep dive — it's interesting).
- Media attachments (image/video/audio) — I'll sketch the upload path.
- End-to-end encryption — I'll state where keys live, not build the full protocol.
- Message search, reactions, threads, calls.

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Low latency:** p99 message delivery **< 200ms** sender-to-receiver when both online (same region).
- **Durability:** a message accepted by the server is **never lost** — RPO ≈ 0 for accepted messages.
- **Ordering & consistency:** messages within a conversation appear in a **consistent order for all participants**; I'll argue this is *per-conversation strong ordering*, not global strong consistency.
- **Availability:** **99.99%** for the messaging path (~52 min/yr down).
- **Scale:** ~**2B users**, ~**100B messages/day**, hundreds of millions concurrent connections.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** "Highly consistent" in the prompt is in tension with global scale — I'll deliver *per-conversation* ordering and read-your-own-writes, not cross-conversation linearizability, and I'll call that out in the ledger. True E2E encryption also limits server-side features (search, server fan-out logic); I'll note the trade-off rather than fully build Signal protocol.

> 💬 "Chat looks simple but the hard part isn't sending one message — it's guaranteeing ordered, durable delivery to people who may be offline, across the planet, at billions-per-day scale. That's where I'll spend my time."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a chat app like WhatsApp. The three things I absolutely must nail are real-time one-on-one messaging, group messaging, and offline push notifications. Above the line I'm keeping it to those three; presence, read receipts, media, and encryption I'll treat as deep dives or stretch goals so I don't sprawl. Non-functionally, the bar is: messages feel instant — under about 200 milliseconds — they're never lost once we accept them, and everyone in a conversation sees them in the same order. I'll explicitly *not* promise global strong consistency, because at two billion users that's the wrong trade — per-conversation ordering is what users actually perceive."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):**
- **"Do we need true end-to-end encryption?"** — If yes, the server can't read messages, so server-side group fan-out and search change fundamentally; the server becomes a blind relay + key directory.
- **"Max group size?"** — 256 (WhatsApp) vs 100K+ (Discord/Slack channels) flips group delivery from fan-out-on-write to fan-out-on-read. Huge architectural fork.
- **"Message retention?"** — WhatsApp deletes from server after delivery (cheap storage). Slack/Discord keep history forever (massive storage + search). This changes my storage budget by orders of magnitude.
- **"Multi-device per user?"** — Sync across phone+web+desktop adds a per-device cursor and fan-out to N devices, not N users.
- **"Global / multi-region?"** — Yes per the prompt → I need geo-routing and a cross-region story.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:**
- Groups capped at **~500 members** (fan-out-on-write viable; I'll note where it breaks).
- **Persistent history** kept (WhatsApp-style "deliver then delete" mentioned as a cost lever).
- **Multi-device** supported (per-device delivery cursor).
- Messages are **text-dominant**; media goes through a separate blob path.
- E2E encryption **optional** — I'll design the plaintext-at-rest server and note the E2E delta.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are we building WhatsApp-style small encrypted groups, or Discord-style giant channels? I'll assume the former with capped groups, but that's the single biggest fork, so tell me if I should pivot."

Let me now compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions.

content.text:
## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring exhaustive estimation — here are only the numbers that force a design decision. | Metric | Value | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Avg messages/sec | ~1.2M/s | baseline write throughput | | **Peak messages/sec** | **~3.5M/s** | sizes the ingest/queue tier | | Concurrent connections | ~500M | drives the connection-server fleet | | WebSocket servers | ~5,000 (@100K conns each) | the gateway fleet | | **Delivery rows/sec at peak (after group fan-out)** | **~55M/s** | the REAL bottleneck — write amplification | | Storage/day (replicated 3x) | ~90 TB/day | ~33 PB/year → drives "deliver-then-delete" debate | **QPS** *(queries/requests per second — the throughput we must sustain)*. **WebSocket** *(a persistent two-way TCP connection so the server can push to the client without polling — this is how a message reaches you instantly instead of you asking "anything new?" every few seconds)*. > The ONE number that forces the design: **~55M delivery writes/sec at peak**, driven by group fan-out — not the 3.5M raw messages. **Flip threshold:** if average group size or max group size grows past a few hundred, fan-out-on-write collapses and I must switch to fan-out-on-read for large groups. That single number dictates my whole group strategy. 🎙️ **Script:** "Let me get the back-of-envelope out. A hundred billion messages a day is about 1.2 million a second average, call it 3.5 million at peak. That's big but not scary on its own. The scary number is *delivery* — once I fan a group message out to every member, I'm doing roughly 55 million delivery writes a second at peak. So the design isn't bottlenecked on receiving messages, it's bottlenecked on *fanning them out*. Storage is about 90 terabytes a day replicated, which is 33 petabytes a year — that's expensive enough that I'd seriously consider WhatsApp's trick of deleting from the server once everyone's received it. Connection-wise, 500 million concurrent sockets at 100K per box is about 5,000 connection servers." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **User** — an account; has devices, presence status. - **Device** — a single phone/web/desktop session; each has its own delivery cursor (multi-device). - **Conversation (Chat)** — a 1:1 or group thread; the unit of ordering. Has a list of members. - **Message** — one text/media item in a conversation; immutable once sent; carries an ordering key. - **Membership** — links a user to a conversation (role, joined-at, last-read pointer). - **Inbox / Delivery entry** — per-recipient-device pointer to undelivered/recent messages (the fan-out target). - **Attachment** — media blob stored separately, referenced by URL/key. 🎙️ **Script:** "My key entities are User, Device, Conversation, Message, Membership, and an Inbox or delivery entry per recipient device. The Conversation is the important one — it's my unit of ordering, every message belongs to exactly one conversation, and that's what lets me promise consistent ordering without a global clock. I'm keeping fields loose for now and I'll firm them up in the data model." ## 5. API / Interface Most traffic rides the **WebSocket** for low latency; REST handles setup and history. The caller is always identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token proving who you are, so we never trust a client-supplied user id)*. **Send a message (1:1 and group)** — over WebSocket (falls back to REST POST): ``` WS frame: { type:"SEND", convId, clientMsgId (UUID), text, attachments[] } Server ack:{ type:"ACK", clientMsgId, serverMsgId, seq, ts } ``` - *Why:* WebSocket gives push-grade latency. **clientMsgId is the idempotency key** — retries return the same serverMsgId, so a flaky network never duplicates a message. **Receive a message** — server pushes: ``` WS frame: { type:"MSG", convId, serverMsgId, seq, senderId, text, ts } Client ack:{ type:"DELIVERED", convId, seq } // moves the delivery cursor ``` **Fetch history** (cold start / new device): ``` GET /v1/conversations/{convId}/messages?before={seq}&limit=50 -> page of messages ``` - *Why GET:* pure read, pageable, cacheable. **Create / manage conversation:** ``` POST /v1/conversations { type:"group", memberIds[] } -> convId POST /v1/conversations/{id}/members { userId } ``` **Notifications / presence:** ``` WS frame: { type:"PRESENCE", status:"online|away" } // heartbeat POST /v1/devices { pushToken, platform } // register for APNs/FCM ``` > 💬 "I'll run the hot send/receive path over a WebSocket for latency, and keep REST for history fetch and conversation setup. The crucial detail is the clientMsgId — that's my idempotency key, so a retry over a dropped connection never creates a duplicate message." 🎙️ **Script:** "The send and receive path lives on a persistent WebSocket so we can push instantly both ways. When you send, you generate a client message id — a UUID — and that doubles as my idempotency key: if your phone retries because it didn't see the ack, the server recognizes the id and returns the original instead of writing a duplicate. The server replies with an authoritative serverMsgId and a sequence number, which is what guarantees ordering. History fetch and creating groups are plain REST because they're not latency-critical." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Users can send & receive 1:1 messages in real time" The minimal slice: a client holds a WebSocket to a **connection server**; messages get persisted and pushed to the recipient's connection. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients A[Sender app] B[Receiver app] end A -->|1 WS SEND| GW[Connection server
WebSocket gateway] GW -->|2 write| MS[Message service] MS -->|3 persist| DB[(Message DB
sharded by convId)] MS -->|4 lookup conn| REG[(Session registry
userId to server)] MS -->|5 route| GW2[Connection server
of receiver] GW2 -->|6 WS push| B MS -->|2b ack| GW GW -->|ack| A classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class A,B,GW,GW2,REG hot; class MS,DB cold; ``` **Flow:** 1. Sender pushes `SEND` over its WebSocket to the **connection server** *(a stateful box that just holds millions of open sockets and shuttles frames — it owns no business logic)*. 2. Gateway forwards to the **Message service**, which assigns a **serverMsgId + per-conversation sequence number** (state change: message now has authoritative order). 3. Message persisted to the **Message DB** (durable — RPO 0 from here). 4. Message service consults the **Session registry** *(a fast key-value map of userId→which connection server holds their live socket; in Redis)* to find the receiver. 5. Routes the frame to the receiver's connection server. 6. That server pushes `MSG` down the receiver's socket. Sender gets its `ACK` in parallel. > 💬 "Connection servers are dumb pipes that hold the sockets. The Message service is the brain — it persists, assigns the sequence number, and uses a Redis session registry to find which box holds the recipient's socket, then routes to it." ### 6.2 "Users can send & receive group messages" New requirement: one message must reach N members. I introduce **fan-out** and a per-recipient **Inbox**. For offline members, I need durable per-device delivery state. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sender] -->|1 WS SEND| GW[Connection server] GW -->|2| MS[Message service] MS -->|3 persist| DB[(Message DB
by convId)] MS -->|4 enqueue| Q[[Fan-out queue
Kafka]] Q --> FO[Fan-out workers] FO -->|5 read members| MEM[(Membership DB)] FO -->|6 write inbox rows| IDB[(Inbox DB
by userId+device)] FO -->|7 route to online| GW2[Connection servers] GW2 -->|8 push| R[Online members] FO -->|7b offline| NS[Notification service] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class A,GW,GW2,R hot; class MS,DB,Q,FO,MEM,IDB,NS cold; ``` **Flow:** 1–3. Same as 1:1 — persist once, assign sequence. 4. Message service enqueues a fan-out job onto **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers the firehose so nothing is lost if workers fall behind — it decouples "message accepted" from "message delivered to everyone")*. 5–6. **Fan-out workers** read group membership and write a lightweight **Inbox row** per recipient device (pointer to the message, not a copy). 7. For online members, route straight to their connection server (step 8); for offline ones, hand to the Notification service. > 💬 "The message body is stored once in the Message DB. Fan-out only writes tiny pointer rows into each recipient's inbox — that's what makes the 55-million-writes-a-second number survivable, and Kafka absorbs the burst so a slow worker never drops a message." ### 6.3 "Users receive push notifications when offline" ```mermaid flowchart TD FO[Fan-out worker] -->|1 recipient offline?| REG[(Session registry)] FO -->|2 yes -> notify| NS[Notification service] NS -->|3 lookup token| TOK[(Device token DB)] NS -->|4 send| APNs[APNs / FCM] APNs -->|5 push| Phone[Offline device] Phone -->|6 reopens app, WS connect| GW[Connection server] GW -->|7 pull missed| IDB[(Inbox DB)] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class GW,Phone hot; class FO,REG,NS,TOK,APNs,IDB cold; ``` **Flow:** When fan-out sees no live socket in the **Session registry**, it calls the **Notification service**, which looks up the device's push token and hands off to **APNs/FCM** *(Apple/Google's push gateways — the only way to wake a backgrounded phone)*. When the user reopens the app, the client reconnects and **pulls everything since its last delivery cursor** from the Inbox DB. **Final (high-level):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients C[Apps web mobile] end C -->|WS| LB[L4 load balancer] LB --> GW[Connection servers
500M sockets / 5K boxes] GW --> MS[Message service
assigns seq, persists] MS --> DB[(Message DB
shard by convId · cols: convId,seq,senderId,body,ts)] MS --> REG[(Session registry
Redis userId to server)] MS --> Q[[Kafka fan-out log]] Q --> FO[Fan-out workers] FO --> MEM[(Membership DB)] FO --> IDB[(Inbox DB
shard by userId · cols: userId,deviceId,convId,seq,delivered)] FO --> GW FO --> NS[Notification service] --> APNs[APNs / FCM] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class C,LB,GW,REG hot; class MS,DB,Q,FO,MEM,IDB,NS,APNs cold; ``` 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: your app holds a WebSocket to a connection server. You send; the Message service persists the message once, stamps it with a per-conversation sequence number, and acks you. Then it drops a fan-out job on Kafka. Workers read the group's members and write a tiny pointer into each recipient's inbox — online folks get pushed immediately via the session registry, offline folks get a push notification through APNs or FCM and pull the backlog when they reopen. The body's stored once; everything else is pointers and routing." > 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The three juiciest deep dives are guaranteed ordered delivery, presence at 500M connections, and group fan-out economics. Which do you want first — or is there something else you're probing for?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store & why | |---|---|---| | **Message** | convId (PK shard), seq, msgId, senderId, body, ts, type | **Wide-column (Cassandra/ScyllaDB)** — partition by convId, clustered by seq → a conversation's history is one contiguous, append-only read. | | **Conversation** | convId, type, createdAt | **Cassandra/SQL** — small, read on open. | | **Membership** | convId, userId, role, lastReadSeq | **Cassandra** — queried both convId→members (fan-out) and userId→convs (chat list); denormalize both ways. | | **Inbox / Delivery** | userId (shard), deviceId, convId, seq, delivered | **Cassandra/KV** — partition by userId; high write volume, point reads. | | **Session registry** | userId → {server, deviceId} | **Redis** — ephemeral, sub-ms, TTL'd by heartbeat. | | **Device token** | deviceId, pushToken, platform | **SQL/KV** — read on notify. | | **Attachment** | blobId, url, size, mime | **S3/blob + CDN**; DB holds only the reference. | **Why wide-column for messages:** *(Cassandra/ScyllaDB — a write-optimized store using an LSM-tree, which batches writes in memory and flushes sequentially to disk, so it eats millions of writes/sec; perfect for an append-heavy message log, and partitioning by convId co-locates a conversation for fast ordered reads)*. SQL would buckle on ~3.5M writes/s and PB-scale. 🎙️ **Script:** "Messages go in a wide-column store like Cassandra, partitioned by conversation id and clustered by sequence number — so a conversation is a contiguous append-only stripe that I can page through cheaply, and the LSM-tree design swallows millions of writes a second, which SQL just can't. The session registry — who's connected where — lives in Redis because it's ephemeral and needs sub-millisecond lookups. Media never touches the database; it goes to blob storage behind a CDN and I only store the reference." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great > 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "a message must be ordered, durable, and delivered exactly once as the user perceives it" and reason forward from there. ### How do we guarantee ordered, exactly-once-feeling delivery? **#### Bad: trust client timestamps for ordering.** **Approach:** each message carries the sender's clock; recipients sort by it. **Challenges:** clocks are skewed and forgeable. ⚠️ **Trap:** clock skew reorders a conversation; two phones disagree on what was said first. Sinks you instantly. **#### Good: server-assigned global timestamp.** **Approach:** the Message service stamps each message with server time. **Challenges:** across sharded message servers, "global time" still drifts and ties happen at scale; doesn't give a gap-free order. **#### Great: per-conversation monotonic sequence number.** **Approach:** ordering is **scoped to the conversation**, not global. The shard owning a convId hands out a strictly increasing `seq` (a counter in the partition). Clients render by `seq`; gaps trigger a re-fetch. Idempotency via `clientMsgId` dedup. Delivery is **at-least-once** on the wire (we retry until DELIVERED ack), and the client **dedupes by msgId**, so the user *perceives* exactly-once. **Trade-off:** no cross-conversation total order — but users never perceive that, so it's the right scope. This is what real systems do. > 🧠 **If they ask "why not one global sequencer?":** "A single global counter at 3.5M/s is a bottleneck and a single point of failure. Ordering only matters *within* a conversation, so I scope the counter to the conversation shard — billions of independent tiny counters, no contention." 🎙️ **Script:** "Ordering is scoped per conversation, not globally. The shard that owns a conversation hands out a strictly increasing sequence number, clients render in that order, and if they spot a gap they re-fetch. On the wire I'm at-least-once — I retry until I get a delivered ack — but the client dedupes on message id, so to the user it's exactly-once. I deliberately don't promise a global order across all conversations because nobody can perceive that, and trying to would cost me a central bottleneck." ### How do we deliver to offline users without losing messages? (the durable inbox) **#### Good: store message, mark recipient online/offline, push only if online.** **Challenges:** race — user goes offline *between* the check and the push; message vanishes. **#### Great: durable per-device Inbox cursor + pull-on-reconnect.** **Approach:** every message fans out a durable Inbox pointer per device with a `delivered` flag. Online → push immediately AND keep the pointer until the DELIVERED ack moves the cursor. Offline → pointer waits; on reconnect the client sends its `last-seq` and pulls everything after it. Push notification is a *best-effort wakeup*, never the source of truth. ```mermaid flowchart TD M[New message seq=42] --> I[(Inbox: device cursor=40)] I -->|online| P[Push seq 41,42] P -->|DELIVERED ack| I2[(cursor=42)] I -->|offline| W[Pointer waits] W -->|reconnect, last-seq=40| Pull[Pull 41,42] --> I2 classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class M,P,Pull hot; ``` > 🧠 **If they ask about WhatsApp's storage:** "WhatsApp deletes the body from the server once all devices ack delivery — the inbox is the queue, not permanent history. That's how they avoid my 33-PB/year bill. Slack keeps everything, so they pay for it. It's a product decision." 🎙️ **Script:** "Delivery is driven by a durable per-device cursor, not by an online flag. Every recipient device has a pointer to the last sequence number it acknowledged. If you're online I push and wait for your delivered ack before advancing the cursor; if you're offline the pointer just sits there, and when you reconnect you tell me your last sequence and I replay everything after it. The push notification is only a doorbell to wake your phone — it's never how the message actually arrives, so losing a push never loses a message." ### How do we track presence (online/offline/away) at 500M connections? **#### Bad: write a DB row on every status change.** **Challenges:** flapping connections = write storm; ⚠️ stale "online" when a socket dies silently. **#### Great: heartbeat + Redis TTL, and only notify interested parties.** **Approach:** the connection server refreshes a Redis key `presence:userId` with a short **TTL** *(time-to-live — the key auto-expires if not refreshed, so a dead socket goes offline automatically without anyone writing "offline")* every ~30s. Presence is read on-demand (opening a chat) or pushed only to a user's **contacts/active conversations**, not broadcast globally — fan-out of presence is itself a scaling trap for big follower counts. **Trade-off:** up to one TTL window of staleness (~30s) — fine for presence, which is inherently fuzzy. > 🧠 **If they ask "why TTL not explicit offline event?":** "Sockets die silently — laptop lid closes, no FIN packet. A TTL means absence of a heartbeat *is* the offline signal; I don't depend on a clean disconnect I might never get." 🎙️ **Script:** "Presence is a Redis key with a 30-second TTL that the connection server keeps refreshing while your socket's alive. If your connection dies — even silently, like a laptop lid closing — the key just expires and you're offline, no explicit event needed. I only propagate presence to people who care, your contacts or open conversations, because broadcasting presence for someone with a million contacts is its own fan-out disaster. Thirty seconds of staleness is totally fine for a green dot." ### How do we handle group fan-out — the 55M-writes/s bottleneck? **#### Bad: fan-out-on-write for ALL groups.** **Challenges:** a 100K-member group = 100K writes per message; a single huge/active group becomes a **hot partition** and melts the inbox shard. ⚠️ **Trap:** the "celebrity/megagroup" problem. **#### Great: hybrid — fan-out-on-write for small groups, fan-out-on-read for large.** **Approach:** below a threshold (~500 members) push pointers to each inbox (fast reads, cheap). Above it, **don't fan out** — members **pull** from the conversation's message log when they open it (one read of a hot, well-cached partition instead of 100K writes). Threshold is tunable. **Bottleneck relief:** caps write amplification; the new ceiling is read QPS on a hot conversation partition, which I relieve with a read replica + cache for that partition. ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Group message] --> D{members > 500?} D -->|no| W[Fan-out-on-write
pointer per inbox] D -->|yes| R[Write once to conv log
members pull on open] W --> Push[Push to online] R --> Cache[(Hot conv cache + replicas)] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class Push,Cache hot; class W,R cold; ``` > 🧠 **If they ask "why not always fan-out-on-read?":** "For small active 1:1s and tiny groups, on-read means every open hammers shared partitions and you lose the cheap push path. Hybrid gives instant push for the common case and protects against megagroups." 🎙️ **Script:** "Group fan-out is the real bottleneck, so I go hybrid. Small groups — under about 500 — I fan out on write, dropping a tiny pointer into each member's inbox, which gives instant push. Big groups I refuse to fan out; I write the message once to the conversation log and members pull it when they open the channel, backed by a cache and read replicas on that hot partition. That single threshold is what turns an impossible 55-million-writes-a-second into something survivable, and it directly handles the megagroup hot-partition trap." ### Where does end-to-end encryption fit? **#### Great (delta, not full build):** clients hold key pairs; the server runs a **key directory** (public keys per device) and becomes a **blind relay** — it stores and routes ciphertext it can't read. Group messages use sender-keys so the sender encrypts once per group. **Trade-off:** server-side search, server-driven fan-out logic, and multi-device sync all get harder (keys must be synced across devices). I'd flag that E2E and rich server features are fundamentally in tension. > 🧠 **If they ask:** "I wouldn't reinvent crypto — I'd use the Signal protocol (double ratchet). The server's job shrinks to a key directory plus ciphertext relay; everything in my design still works, the body's just opaque." 🎙️ **Script:** "For end-to-end encryption I'd lean on the Signal protocol rather than rolling my own. The server turns into a blind relay plus a public-key directory — it routes ciphertext it can't read. My whole architecture still holds; the message body's just opaque. The honest cost is that server-side features like search and clean multi-device sync get much harder, and I'd call that trade-off out rather than pretend it's free." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost - **Availability:** target **99.99%** on the messaging path via multi-AZ connection fleets behind L4 LBs and a replicated Kafka + Cassandra (RF=3, quorum writes). - **Graceful degradation:** - *Connection server dies* → client auto-reconnects to another (sessions are stateless beyond the socket; registry TTL cleans up); pulls backlog via cursor. - *Kafka backs up* → messages still persisted and acked; fan-out just lags (delivery delayed, not lost). - *Notification provider (APNs) down* → message still in inbox; user gets it on next open; no data loss. - *Redis presence down* → assume "maybe offline," fall back to push notification. - **RPO/RTO:** accepted messages **RPO ≈ 0** (quorum-replicated before ack); RTO for a region failover ~minutes via DNS/anycast reroute. Inbox cursors are durable so no re-delivery storms. Tested restores monthly from Cassandra snapshots. - **Multi-region:** users pinned to a home region; cross-region conversations replicate the message log async (eventual cross-region, strong within region). Failover triggers on regional health-check breach. - **Cost:** dominant driver is **storage + cross-region bandwidth** (~33 PB/yr replicated, ~$ millions/yr) — this is exactly why "deliver-then-delete" is on the table; connection fleet (~5K boxes) is secondary. - **Observability:** SLO **burn-rate alerts** on delivery latency p99 and undelivered-backlog depth; distributed tracing on the send→fan-out→deliver path; runbooks for "fan-out lag spiking." 🎙️ **Script:** "The golden rule is: once I ack a message it's quorum-replicated, so RPO is essentially zero — every failure downstream of that delays delivery but never loses data. Connection servers are disposable; if one dies you reconnect elsewhere and pull your backlog from your cursor. Kafka backing up, APNs down, Redis down — all degrade to 'you get it a bit later,' never 'it's gone.' My biggest cost is storage and cross-region bandwidth, which is exactly why I'd consider deleting delivered messages from the server." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger | Decision | Gave up | What would reverse it | |---|---|---| | **Per-conversation ordering, not global** | Cross-conversation total order | If a feature needed a global event timeline (it won't for chat) — tied to my "consistency = per-conversation" SLO. | | **Hybrid fan-out (write small / read large)** | Uniform code path; some complexity | If max group size were capped low (<256), pure fan-out-on-write is simpler; if all groups were huge, pure on-read. | | **Keep full history (vs deliver-then-delete)** | ~33 PB/yr storage cost | If we're WhatsApp-style and don't need server history, delete on delivery and slash the bill — flips on the retention answer. | 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'm least dogmatic about: scoping ordering per conversation, the hybrid fan-out threshold, and whether to keep history at all. Each flips on an answer I'd want from you — how consistent we really need to be, how big groups get, and how long we retain messages. Give me those three and I'll commit harder." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. How do you guarantee message ordering?** Per-conversation monotonic sequence numbers assigned by the shard owning the conversation; clients render by seq and re-fetch on gaps. I don't attempt global ordering because it requires a central sequencer that bottlenecks at 3.5M/s and users can't perceive it anyway. Within a conversation that gives a gap-free, consistent order for everyone. > 💬 "Ordering is scoped to the conversation — a per-conversation counter — not global, because that's all the user can perceive and it avoids a central bottleneck." **2. What if the same message is delivered twice?** Wire delivery is at-least-once (we retry until a DELIVERED ack), so duplicates are possible on the network. The client dedupes on the immutable msgId/clientMsgId, so the user sees each message once. The clientMsgId also makes the *send* idempotent, so a retried send never creates a second message server-side. > 💬 "At-least-once on the wire, deduped by message id on the client — so the user experiences exactly-once." **3. A user is offline for a week, then opens the app. What happens?** Their inbox cursor still points at the last seq they acked; on reconnect they send last-seq and we replay everything after it, paginated. If we use deliver-then-delete, undelivered messages were retained precisely because they weren't acked. Push notifications during that week were best-effort doorbells, irrelevant to correctness. > 💬 "Their durable cursor tells us exactly where they left off; we replay the gap on reconnect." **4. How do you handle a 100,000-member group (megagroup)?** Above a ~500-member threshold I switch from fan-out-on-write to fan-out-on-read: the message is written once to the conversation log and members pull it on open, served from a cached, replicated hot partition. This avoids 100K inbox writes per message that would melt a shard. The trade-off is slightly less "instant" for huge groups, which is acceptable. > 💬 "I stop fanning out past a few hundred members and let big groups pull from a cached conversation log — that defuses the hot-partition bomb." **5. How does presence stay accurate when connections drop silently?** Connection servers refresh a Redis presence key with a ~30s TTL; if the heartbeat stops — even from a silent socket death — the key expires and the user is offline automatically. I never rely on a clean disconnect event because sockets often die without one. Up to ~30s staleness, which is fine for a status dot. > 💬 "Presence is a self-expiring Redis key — absence of a heartbeat *is* the offline signal." **6. How do connection servers find which box holds the recipient's socket?** A Redis session registry maps userId/deviceId → connection server. Fan-out workers and the message service look it up to route a push; if absent, the recipient is offline and we notify. The registry entry is TTL'd and refreshed by the same heartbeat, so it self-heals when a box dies. > 💬 "A Redis map of user-to-server tells us where to route; missing entry means offline, so we push-notify instead." **7. Why Cassandra for messages and not SQL?** The workload is append-heavy at millions of writes/sec and petabytes of data, with the only read pattern being "give me a conversation's messages in order." Cassandra's LSM-tree absorbs that write rate and partitioning by convId makes the read a single contiguous scan. SQL's b-tree writes and single-primary scaling would fall over and sharding it manually reinvents Cassandra. > 💬 "Append-heavy, ordered-by-conversation, petabyte scale — that's exactly Cassandra's sweet spot; SQL would buckle." **8. How do you do read receipts efficiently?** Each membership row carries a lastReadSeq; when a user reads up to seq N, the client sends one update and we fan that out as a lightweight event to other participants, not per-message. For large groups I aggregate (a count, not per-user receipts) to avoid N-squared receipt traffic. It's eventually consistent — receipts are not latency-critical. > 💬 "I track a single last-read sequence per member and broadcast that, not a receipt per message — and aggregate for big groups." **9. How do media attachments work?** The client requests a pre-signed upload URL, uploads directly to blob storage (S3), and the message carries only the blob reference; recipients download via CDN. The body never bloats the message DB and large transfers never touch my message path. We can scan/transcode async. > 💬 "Media goes straight to blob storage via a pre-signed URL behind a CDN; the message only carries a pointer." **10. What's the consistency model across regions?** Strong (per-conversation) within a user's home region; cross-region conversations replicate the log asynchronously, so a participant in another region may see a sub-second lag. I pin users to a home region to keep the common case fast and accept eventual cross-region convergence. A globally synchronous write would add 100ms+ per hop and kill latency. > 💬 "Strong within a region, async across regions — pinning users home keeps the hot path fast and lag is sub-second." **11. How do you prevent abuse / spam at this scale?** Rate-limit sends per user/device at the connection server using a distributed token bucket in Redis (shared state, not per-box, or a user could spread load across boxes). Layer spam-detection async on the message stream and support block/report flows. Auth on every frame via the JWT established at socket handshake. > 💬 "Distributed Redis token-bucket rate limits — shared state so you can't dodge it by hopping boxes — plus async spam scoring." **12. How would you add typing indicators / ephemeral signals?** Route them over the WebSocket as fire-and-forget frames that are NOT persisted and NOT fanned out durably — they go straight through the connection servers to online participants only. Persisting them would 10x my write load for data nobody re-reads. They're best-effort by design. > 💬 "Typing indicators ride the socket as ephemeral, unpersisted frames to online users only — never written to the DB." **13. What happens when a whole region goes down?** Health checks trip and DNS/anycast reroutes affected users to a neighboring region where their replicated message log lives; they reconnect and resume from their cursor. RPO is near-zero for cross-region-replicated conversations and RTO is minutes. In-flight, un-replicated messages are the only risk, which quorum-within-region minimizes. > 💬 "Anycast reroutes users to a healthy region with their replicated log; they reconnect on their cursor with near-zero data loss." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "I'm designing WhatsApp-scale chat: a hundred billion messages a day, half a billion concurrent connections. Clients hold WebSockets to dumb connection servers; a Message service persists each message once, stamps it with a per-conversation sequence number for ordering, and acks. Fan-out workers off a Kafka log write tiny pointers into each recipient's durable inbox — online users get pushed via a Redis session registry, offline users get an APNs/FCM doorbell and pull their backlog on reconnect using a per-device cursor. The bottleneck isn't ingesting messages, it's fan-out at ~55 million writes a second, so I go hybrid: fan-out-on-write for small groups, fan-out-on-read for megagroups. Messages live in Cassandra partitioned by conversation, media in blob storage behind a CDN, presence as a self-expiring Redis key. Once I ack, RPO is zero — every downstream failure delays delivery but never loses a message. The trade-offs I'd revisit are per-conversation versus global ordering, the fan-out threshold, and whether to keep history or delete on delivery."

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — message delivery, online presence, and group fan-out — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in a chat system: **a message must feel instant (sub-100ms delivery) yet never be lost** — so we're balancing low-latency real-time push against durable, ordered, exactly-once-feeling persistence.

🏢 I'll target a **Meta/WhatsApp-style bar** (massive scale, read/delivery hot-path obsession). Tell me if it's a Slack/Discord-style workspace product and I'll re-tilt toward channels and rich presence.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Users should be able to send & receive 1:1 messages in real time** (the heart of it).
- **Users should be able to send & receive group messages.**
- **Users should receive push notifications for new messages when offline.**

**Below the line (out of scope for the main pass, will mention):**
- Read receipts / delivery receipts (I'll touch in deep dives).
- Online/offline/away presence (I'll design it as a deep dive — it's interesting).
- Media attachments (image/video/audio) — I'll sketch the upload path.
- End-to-end encryption — I'll state where keys live, not build the full protocol.
- Message search, reactions, threads, calls.

**Non-Functional (talkable targets):**
- **Low latency:** p99 message delivery **< 200ms** sender-to-receiver when both online (same region).
- **Durability:** a message accepted by the server is **never lost** — RPO ≈ 0 for accepted messages.
- **Ordering & consistency:** messages within a conversation appear in a **consistent order for all participants**; I'll argue this is *per-conversation strong ordering*, not global strong consistency.
- **Availability:** **99.99%** for the messaging path (~52 min/yr down).
- **Scale:** ~**2B users**, ~**100B messages/day**, hundreds of millions concurrent connections.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** "Highly consistent" in the prompt is in tension with global scale — I'll deliver *per-conversation* ordering and read-your-own-writes, not cross-conversation linearizability, and I'll call that out in the ledger. True E2E encryption also limits server-side features (search, server fan-out logic); I'll note the trade-off rather than fully build Signal protocol.

> 💬 "Chat looks simple but the hard part isn't sending one message — it's guaranteeing ordered, durable delivery to people who may be offline, across the planet, at billions-per-day scale. That's where I'll spend my time."

🎙️ **Script:** "So this is a chat app like WhatsApp. The three things I absolutely must nail are real-time one-on-one messaging, group messaging, and offline push notifications. Above the line I'm keeping it to those three; presence, read receipts, media, and encryption I'll treat as deep dives or stretch goals so I don't sprawl. Non-functionally, the bar is: messages feel instant — under about 200 milliseconds — they're never lost once we accept them, and everyone in a conversation sees them in the same order. I'll explicitly *not* promise global strong consistency, because at two billion users that's the wrong trade — per-conversation ordering is what users actually perceive."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Questions I'd ask (and why each changes the design):**
- **"Do we need true end-to-end encryption?"** — If yes, the server can't read messages, so server-side group fan-out and search change fundamentally; the server becomes a blind relay + key directory.
- **"Max group size?"** — 256 (WhatsApp) vs 100K+ (Discord/Slack channels) flips group delivery from fan-out-on-write to fan-out-on-read. Huge architectural fork.
- **"Message retention?"** — WhatsApp deletes from server after delivery (cheap storage). Slack/Discord keep history forever (massive storage + search). This changes my storage budget by orders of magnitude.
- **"Multi-device per user?"** — Sync across phone+web+desktop adds a per-device cursor and fan-out to N devices, not N users.
- **"Global / multi-region?"** — Yes per the prompt → I need geo-routing and a cross-region story.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:**
- Groups capped at **~500 members** (fan-out-on-write viable; I'll note where it breaks).
- **Persistent history** kept (WhatsApp-style "deliver then delete" mentioned as a cost lever).
- **Multi-device** supported (per-device delivery cursor).
- Messages are **text-dominant**; media goes through a separate blob path.
- E2E encryption **optional** — I'll design the plaintext-at-rest server and note the E2E delta.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Before I size it — are we building WhatsApp-style small encrypted groups, or Discord-style giant channels? I'll assume the former with capped groups, but that's the single biggest fork, so tell me if I should pivot."

Let me now compute the few numbers that actually drive decisions.

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) I'm deferring exhaustive estimation — here are only the numbers that force a design decision. | Metric | Value | Why it matters | |---|---|---| | Avg messages/sec | ~1.2M/s | baseline write throughput | | **Peak messages/sec** | **~3.5M/s** | sizes the ingest/queue tier | | Concurrent connections | ~500M | drives the connection-server fleet | | WebSocket servers | ~5,000 (@100K conns each) | the gateway fleet | | **Delivery rows/sec at peak (after group fan-out)** | **~55M/s** | the REAL bottleneck — write amplification | | Storage/day (replicated 3x) | ~90 TB/day | ~33 PB/year → drives "deliver-then-delete" debate | **QPS** *(queries/requests per second — the throughput we must sustain)*. **WebSocket** *(a persistent two-way TCP connection so the server can push to the client without polling — this is how a message reaches you instantly instead of you asking "anything new?" every few seconds)*. > The ONE number that forces the design: **~55M delivery writes/sec at peak**, driven by group fan-out — not the 3.5M raw messages. **Flip threshold:** if average group size or max group size grows past a few hundred, fan-out-on-write collapses and I must switch to fan-out-on-read for large groups. That single number dictates my whole group strategy. 🎙️ **Script:** "Let me get the back-of-envelope out. A hundred billion messages a day is about 1.2 million a second average, call it 3.5 million at peak. That's big but not scary on its own. The scary number is *delivery* — once I fan a group message out to every member, I'm doing roughly 55 million delivery writes a second at peak. So the design isn't bottlenecked on receiving messages, it's bottlenecked on *fanning them out*. Storage is about 90 terabytes a day replicated, which is 33 petabytes a year — that's expensive enough that I'd seriously consider WhatsApp's trick of deleting from the server once everyone's received it. Connection-wise, 500 million concurrent sockets at 100K per box is about 5,000 connection servers." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **User** — an account; has devices, presence status. - **Device** — a single phone/web/desktop session; each has its own delivery cursor (multi-device). - **Conversation (Chat)** — a 1:1 or group thread; the unit of ordering. Has a list of members. - **Message** — one text/media item in a conversation; immutable once sent; carries an ordering key. - **Membership** — links a user to a conversation (role, joined-at, last-read pointer). - **Inbox / Delivery entry** — per-recipient-device pointer to undelivered/recent messages (the fan-out target). - **Attachment** — media blob stored separately, referenced by URL/key. 🎙️ **Script:** "My key entities are User, Device, Conversation, Message, Membership, and an Inbox or delivery entry per recipient device. The Conversation is the important one — it's my unit of ordering, every message belongs to exactly one conversation, and that's what lets me promise consistent ordering without a global clock. I'm keeping fields loose for now and I'll firm them up in the data model." ## 5. API / Interface Most traffic rides the **WebSocket** for low latency; REST handles setup and history. The caller is always identified from a **JWT** *(a signed token proving who you are, so we never trust a client-supplied user id)*. **Send a message (1:1 and group)** — over WebSocket (falls back to REST POST): ``` WS frame: { type:"SEND", convId, clientMsgId (UUID), text, attachments[] } Server ack:{ type:"ACK", clientMsgId, serverMsgId, seq, ts } ``` - *Why:* WebSocket gives push-grade latency. **clientMsgId is the idempotency key** — retries return the same serverMsgId, so a flaky network never duplicates a message. **Receive a message** — server pushes: ``` WS frame: { type:"MSG", convId, serverMsgId, seq, senderId, text, ts } Client ack:{ type:"DELIVERED", convId, seq } // moves the delivery cursor ``` **Fetch history** (cold start / new device): ``` GET /v1/conversations/{convId}/messages?before={seq}&limit=50 -> page of messages ``` - *Why GET:* pure read, pageable, cacheable. **Create / manage conversation:** ``` POST /v1/conversations { type:"group", memberIds[] } -> convId POST /v1/conversations/{id}/members { userId } ``` **Notifications / presence:** ``` WS frame: { type:"PRESENCE", status:"online|away" } // heartbeat POST /v1/devices { pushToken, platform } // register for APNs/FCM ``` > 💬 "I'll run the hot send/receive path over a WebSocket for latency, and keep REST for history fetch and conversation setup. The crucial detail is the clientMsgId — that's my idempotency key, so a retry over a dropped connection never creates a duplicate message." 🎙️ **Script:** "The send and receive path lives on a persistent WebSocket so we can push instantly both ways. When you send, you generate a client message id — a UUID — and that doubles as my idempotency key: if your phone retries because it didn't see the ack, the server recognizes the id and returns the original instead of writing a duplicate. The server replies with an authoritative serverMsgId and a sequence number, which is what guarantees ordering. History fetch and creating groups are plain REST because they're not latency-critical." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Users can send & receive 1:1 messages in real time" The minimal slice: a client holds a WebSocket to a **connection server**; messages get persisted and pushed to the recipient's connection. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients A[Sender app] B[Receiver app] end A -->|1 WS SEND| GW[Connection server
WebSocket gateway] GW -->|2 write| MS[Message service] MS -->|3 persist| DB[(Message DB
sharded by convId)] MS -->|4 lookup conn| REG[(Session registry
userId to server)] MS -->|5 route| GW2[Connection server
of receiver] GW2 -->|6 WS push| B MS -->|2b ack| GW GW -->|ack| A classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class A,B,GW,GW2,REG hot; class MS,DB cold; ``` **Flow:** 1. Sender pushes `SEND` over its WebSocket to the **connection server** *(a stateful box that just holds millions of open sockets and shuttles frames — it owns no business logic)*. 2. Gateway forwards to the **Message service**, which assigns a **serverMsgId + per-conversation sequence number** (state change: message now has authoritative order). 3. Message persisted to the **Message DB** (durable — RPO 0 from here). 4. Message service consults the **Session registry** *(a fast key-value map of userId→which connection server holds their live socket; in Redis)* to find the receiver. 5. Routes the frame to the receiver's connection server. 6. That server pushes `MSG` down the receiver's socket. Sender gets its `ACK` in parallel. > 💬 "Connection servers are dumb pipes that hold the sockets. The Message service is the brain — it persists, assigns the sequence number, and uses a Redis session registry to find which box holds the recipient's socket, then routes to it." ### 6.2 "Users can send & receive group messages" New requirement: one message must reach N members. I introduce **fan-out** and a per-recipient **Inbox**. For offline members, I need durable per-device delivery state. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sender] -->|1 WS SEND| GW[Connection server] GW -->|2| MS[Message service] MS -->|3 persist| DB[(Message DB
by convId)] MS -->|4 enqueue| Q[[Fan-out queue
Kafka]] Q --> FO[Fan-out workers] FO -->|5 read members| MEM[(Membership DB)] FO -->|6 write inbox rows| IDB[(Inbox DB
by userId+device)] FO -->|7 route to online| GW2[Connection servers] GW2 -->|8 push| R[Online members] FO -->|7b offline| NS[Notification service] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class A,GW,GW2,R hot; class MS,DB,Q,FO,MEM,IDB,NS cold; ``` **Flow:** 1–3. Same as 1:1 — persist once, assign sequence. 4. Message service enqueues a fan-out job onto **Kafka** *(a durable append-only log that buffers the firehose so nothing is lost if workers fall behind — it decouples "message accepted" from "message delivered to everyone")*. 5–6. **Fan-out workers** read group membership and write a lightweight **Inbox row** per recipient device (pointer to the message, not a copy). 7. For online members, route straight to their connection server (step 8); for offline ones, hand to the Notification service. > 💬 "The message body is stored once in the Message DB. Fan-out only writes tiny pointer rows into each recipient's inbox — that's what makes the 55-million-writes-a-second number survivable, and Kafka absorbs the burst so a slow worker never drops a message." ### 6.3 "Users receive push notifications when offline" ```mermaid flowchart TD FO[Fan-out worker] -->|1 recipient offline?| REG[(Session registry)] FO -->|2 yes -> notify| NS[Notification service] NS -->|3 lookup token| TOK[(Device token DB)] NS -->|4 send| APNs[APNs / FCM] APNs -->|5 push| Phone[Offline device] Phone -->|6 reopens app, WS connect| GW[Connection server] GW -->|7 pull missed| IDB[(Inbox DB)] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class GW,Phone hot; class FO,REG,NS,TOK,APNs,IDB cold; ``` **Flow:** When fan-out sees no live socket in the **Session registry**, it calls the **Notification service**, which looks up the device's push token and hands off to **APNs/FCM** *(Apple/Google's push gateways — the only way to wake a backgrounded phone)*. When the user reopens the app, the client reconnects and **pulls everything since its last delivery cursor** from the Inbox DB. **Final (high-level):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients C[Apps web mobile] end C -->|WS| LB[L4 load balancer] LB --> GW[Connection servers

Redis userId to server)] MS --> Q[[Kafka fan-out log]] Q --> FO[Fan-out workers] FO --> MEM[(Membership DB)] FO --> IDB[(Inbox DB

You send; the Message service persists the message once, stamps it with a per-conversation sequence number, and acks you. Then it drops a fan-out job on Kafka. Workers read the group's members and write a tiny pointer into each recipient's inbox — online folks get pushed immediately via the session registry, offline folks get a push notification through APNs or FCM and pull the backlog when they reopen. The body's stored once; everything else is pointers and routing." > 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The three juiciest deep dives are guaranteed ordered delivery, presence at 500M connections, and group fan-out economics. Which do you want first — or is there something else you're probing for?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store & why | |---|---|---| | **Message** | convId (PK shard), seq, msgId, senderId, body, ts, type | **Wide-column (Cassandra/ScyllaDB)** — partition by convId, clustered by seq → a conversation's history is one contiguous, append-only read. | | **Conversation** | convId, type, createdAt | **Cassandra/SQL** — small, read on open. | | **Membership** | convId, userId, role, lastReadSeq | **Cassandra** — queried both convId→members (fan-out) and userId→convs (chat list); denormalize both ways. | | **Inbox / Delivery** | userId (shard), deviceId, convId, seq, delivered | **Cassandra/KV** — partition by userId; high write volume, point reads. | | **Session registry** | userId → {server, deviceId} | **Redis** — ephemeral, sub-ms, TTL'd by heartbeat. | | **Device token** | deviceId, pushToken, platform | **SQL/KV** — read on notify. | | **Attachment** | blobId, url, size, mime | **S3/blob + CDN**; DB holds only the reference. | **Why wide-column for messages:** *(Cassandra/ScyllaDB — a write-optimized store using an LSM-tree, which batches writes in memory and flushes sequentially to disk, so it eats millions of writes/sec; perfect for an append-heavy message log, and partitioning by convId co-locates a conversation for fast ordered reads)*. SQL would buckle on ~3.5M writes/s and PB-scale. 🎙️ **Script:** "Messages go in a wide-column store like Cassandra, partitioned by conversation id and clustered by sequence number — so a conversation is a contiguous append-only stripe that I can page through cheaply, and the LSM-tree design swallows millions of writes a second, which SQL just can't. The session registry — who's connected where — lives in Redis because it's ephemeral and needs sub-millisecond lookups. Media never touches the database; it goes to blob storage behind a CDN and I only store the reference." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great > 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "a message must be ordered, durable, and delivered exactly once as the user perceives it" and reason forward from there. ### How do we guarantee ordered, exactly-once-feeling delivery? **#### Bad: trust client timestamps for ordering.** **Approach:** each message carries the sender's clock; recipients sort by it. **Challenges:** clocks are skewed and forgeable. ⚠️ **Trap:** clock skew reorders a conversation; two phones disagree on what was said first. Sinks you instantly. **#### Good: server-assigned global timestamp.** **Approach:** the Message service stamps each message with server time. **Challenges:** across sharded message servers, "global time" still drifts and ties happen at scale; doesn't give a gap-free order. **#### Great: per-conversation monotonic sequence number.** **Approach:** ordering is **scoped to the conversation**, not global. The shard owning a convId hands out a strictly increasing `seq` (a counter in the partition). Clients render by `seq`; gaps trigger a re-fetch. Idempotency via `clientMsgId` dedup. Delivery is **at-least-once** on the wire (we retry until DELIVERED ack), and the client **dedupes by msgId**, so the user *perceives* exactly-once. **Trade-off:** no cross-conversation total order — but users never perceive that, so it's the right scope. This is what real systems do. > 🧠 **If they ask "why not one global sequencer?":** "A single global counter at 3.5M/s is a bottleneck and a single point of failure. Ordering only matters *within* a conversation, so I scope the counter to the conversation shard — billions of independent tiny counters, no contention." 🎙️ **Script:** "Ordering is scoped per conversation, not globally. The shard that owns a conversation hands out a strictly increasing sequence number, clients render in that order, and if they spot a gap they re-fetch. On the wire I'm at-least-once — I retry until I get a delivered ack — but the client dedupes on message id, so to the user it's exactly-once. I deliberately don't promise a global order across all conversations because nobody can perceive that, and trying to would cost me a central bottleneck." ### How do we deliver to offline users without losing messages? (the durable inbox) **#### Good: store message, mark recipient online/offline, push only if online.** **Challenges:** race — user goes offline *between* the check and the push; message vanishes. **#### Great: durable per-device Inbox cursor + pull-on-reconnect.** **Approach:** every message fans out a durable Inbox pointer per device with a `delivered` flag. Online → push immediately AND keep the pointer until the DELIVERED ack moves the cursor. Offline → pointer waits; on reconnect the client sends its `last-seq` and pulls everything after it. Push notification is a *best-effort wakeup*, never the source of truth. ```mermaid flowchart TD M[New message seq=42] --> I[(Inbox: device cursor=40)] I -->|online| P[Push seq 41,42] P -->|DELIVERED ack| I2[(cursor=42)] I -->|offline| W[Pointer waits] W -->|reconnect, last-seq=40| Pull[Pull 41,42] --> I2 classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class M,P,Pull hot; ``` > 🧠 **If they ask about WhatsApp's storage:** "WhatsApp deletes the body from the server once all devices ack delivery — the inbox is the queue, not permanent history. That's how they avoid my 33-PB/year bill. Slack keeps everything, so they pay for it. It's a product decision." 🎙️ **Script:** "Delivery is driven by a durable per-device cursor, not by an online flag. Every recipient device has a pointer to the last sequence number it acknowledged. If you're online I push and wait for your delivered ack before advancing the cursor; if you're offline the pointer just sits there, and when you reconnect you tell me your last sequence and I replay everything after it. The push notification is only a doorbell to wake your phone — it's never how the message actually arrives, so losing a push never loses a message." ### How do we track presence (online/offline/away) at 500M connections? **#### Bad: write a DB row on every status change.** **Challenges:** flapping connections = write storm; ⚠️ stale "online" when a socket dies silently. **#### Great: heartbeat + Redis TTL, and only notify interested parties.** **Approach:** the connection server refreshes a Redis key `presence:userId` with a short **TTL** *(time-to-live — the key auto-expires if not refreshed, so a dead socket goes offline automatically without anyone writing "offline")* every ~30s. Presence is read on-demand (opening a chat) or pushed only to a user's **contacts/active conversations**, not broadcast globally — fan-out of presence is itself a scaling trap for big follower counts. **Trade-off:** up to one TTL window of staleness (~30s) — fine for presence, which is inherently fuzzy. > 🧠 **If they ask "why TTL not explicit offline event?":** "Sockets die silently — laptop lid closes, no FIN packet. A TTL means absence of a heartbeat *is* the offline signal; I don't depend on a clean disconnect I might never get." 🎙️ **Script:** "Presence is a Redis key with a 30-second TTL that the connection server keeps refreshing while your socket's alive. If your connection dies — even silently, like a laptop lid closing — the key just expires and you're offline, no explicit event needed. I only propagate presence to people who care, your contacts or open conversations, because broadcasting presence for someone with a million contacts is its own fan-out disaster. Thirty seconds of staleness is totally fine for a green dot." ### How do we handle group fan-out — the 55M-writes/s bottleneck? **#### Bad: fan-out-on-write for ALL groups.** **Challenges:** a 100K-member group = 100K writes per message; a single huge/active group becomes a **hot partition** and melts the inbox shard. ⚠️ **Trap:** the "celebrity/megagroup" problem. **#### Great: hybrid — fan-out-on-write for small groups, fan-out-on-read for large.** **Approach:** below a threshold (~500 members) push pointers to each inbox (fast reads, cheap). Above it, **don't fan out** — members **pull** from the conversation's message log when they open it (one read of a hot, well-cached partition instead of 100K writes). Threshold is tunable. **Bottleneck relief:** caps write amplification; the new ceiling is read QPS on a hot conversation partition, which I relieve with a read replica + cache for that partition. ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Group message] --> D{members > 500?} D -->|no| W[Fan-out-on-write
pointer per inbox] D -->|yes| R[Write once to conv log
members pull on open] W --> Push[Push to online] R --> Cache[(Hot conv cache + replicas)] classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class Push,Cache hot; class W,R cold; ``` > 🧠 **If they ask "why not always fan-out-on-read?":** "For small active 1:1s and tiny groups, on-read means every open hammers shared partitions and you lose the cheap push path. Hybrid gives instant push for the common case and protects against megagroups." 🎙️ **Script:** "Group fan-out is the real bottleneck, so I go hybrid. Small groups — under about 500 — I fan out on write, dropping a tiny pointer into each member's inbox, which gives instant push. Big groups I refuse to fan out; I write the message once to the conversation log and members pull it when they open the channel, backed by a cache and read replicas on that hot partition. That single threshold is what turns an impossible 55-million-writes-a-second into something survivable, and it directly handles the megagroup hot-partition trap." ### Where does end-to-end encryption fit? **#### Great (delta, not full build):** clients hold key pairs; the server runs a **key directory** (public keys per device) and becomes a **blind relay** — it stores and routes ciphertext it can't read. Group messages use sender-keys so the sender encrypts once per group. **Trade-off:** server-side search, server-driven fan-out logic, and multi-device sync all get harder (keys must be synced across devices). I'd flag that E2E and rich server features are fundamentally in tension. > 🧠 **If they ask:** "I wouldn't reinvent crypto — I'd use the Signal protocol (double ratchet). The server's job shrinks to a key directory plus ciphertext relay; everything in my design still works, the body's just opaque." 🎙️ **Script:** "For end-to-end encryption I'd lean on the Signal protocol rather than rolling my own. The server turns into a blind relay plus a public-key directory — it routes ciphertext it can't read. My whole architecture still holds; the message body's just opaque. The honest cost is that server-side features like search and clean multi-device sync get much harder, and I'd call that trade-off out rather than pretend it's free." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost - **Availability:** target **99.99%** on the messaging path via multi-AZ connection fleets behind L4 LBs and a replicated Kafka + Cassandra (RF=3, quorum writes). - **Graceful degradation:** - *Connection server dies* → client auto-reconnects to another (sessions are stateless beyond the socket; registry TTL cleans up); pulls backlog via cursor. - *Kafka backs up* → messages still persisted and acked; fan-out just lags (delivery delayed, not lost). - *Notification provider (APNs) down* → message still in inbox; user gets it on next open; no data loss. - *Redis presence down* → assume "maybe offline," fall back to push notification. - **RPO/RTO:** accepted messages **RPO ≈ 0** (quorum-replicated before ack); RTO for a region failover ~minutes via DNS/anycast reroute. Inbox cursors are durable so no re-delivery storms. Tested restores monthly from Cassandra snapshots. - **Multi-region:** users pinned to a home region; cross-region conversations replicate the message log async (eventual cross-region, strong within region). Failover triggers on regional health-check breach. - **Cost:** dominant driver is **storage + cross-region bandwidth** (~33 PB/yr replicated, ~$ millions/yr) — this is exactly why "deliver-then-delete" is on the table; connection fleet (~5K boxes) is secondary. - **Observability:** SLO **burn-rate alerts** on delivery latency p99 and undelivered-backlog depth; distributed tracing on the send→fan-out→deliver path; runbooks for "fan-out lag spiking." 🎙️ **Script:** "The golden rule is: once I ack a message it's quorum-replicated, so RPO is essentially zero — every failure downstream of that delays delivery but never loses data. Connection servers are disposable; if one dies you reconnect elsewhere and pull your backlog from your cursor. Kafka backing up, APNs down, Redis down — all degrade to 'you get it a bit later,' never 'it's gone.' My biggest cost is storage and cross-region bandwidth, which is exactly why I'd consider deleting delivered messages from the server." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger | Decision | Gave up | What would reverse it | |---|---|---| | **Per-conversation ordering, not global** | Cross-conversation total order | If a feature needed a global event timeline (it won't for chat) — tied to my "consistency = per-conversation" SLO. | | **Hybrid fan-out (write small / read large)** | Uniform code path; some complexity | If max group size were capped low (<256), pure fan-out-on-write is simpler; if all groups were huge, pure on-read. | | **Keep full history (vs deliver-then-delete)** | ~33 PB/yr storage cost | If we're WhatsApp-style and don't need server history, delete on delivery and slash the bill — flips on the retention answer. | 🎙️ **Script:** "The three calls I'm least dogmatic about: scoping ordering per conversation, the hybrid fan-out threshold, and whether to keep history at all. Each flips on an answer I'd want from you — how consistent we really need to be, how big groups get, and how long we retain messages. Give me those three and I'll commit harder." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. How do you guarantee message ordering?** Per-conversation monotonic sequence numbers assigned by the shard owning the conversation; clients render by seq and re-fetch on gaps. I don't attempt global ordering because it requires a central sequencer that bottlenecks at 3.5M/s and users can't perceive it anyway. Within a conversation that gives a gap-free, consistent order for everyone. > 💬 "Ordering is scoped to the conversation — a per-conversation counter — not global, because that's all the user can perceive and it avoids a central bottleneck." **2. What if the same message is delivered twice?** Wire delivery is at-least-once (we retry until a DELIVERED ack), so duplicates are possible on the network. The client dedupes on the immutable msgId/clientMsgId, so the user sees each message once. The clientMsgId also makes the *send* idempotent, so a retried send never creates a second message server-side. > 💬 "At-least-once on the wire, deduped by message id on the client — so the user experiences exactly-once." **3. A user is offline for a week, then opens the app. What happens?** Their inbox cursor still points at the last seq they acked; on reconnect they send last-seq and we replay everything after it, paginated. If we use deliver-then-delete, undelivered messages were retained precisely because they weren't acked. Push notifications during that week were best-effort doorbells, irrelevant to correctness. > 💬 "Their durable cursor tells us exactly where they left off; we replay the gap on reconnect." **4. How do you handle a 100,000-member group (megagroup)?** Above a ~500-member threshold I switch from fan-out-on-write to fan-out-on-read: the message is written once to the conversation log and members pull it on open, served from a cached, replicated hot partition. This avoids 100K inbox writes per message that would melt a shard. The trade-off is slightly less "instant" for huge groups, which is acceptable. > 💬 "I stop fanning out past a few hundred members and let big groups pull from a cached conversation log — that defuses the hot-partition bomb." **5. How does presence stay accurate when connections drop silently?** Connection servers refresh a Redis presence key with a ~30s TTL; if the heartbeat stops — even from a silent socket death — the key expires and the user is offline automatically. I never rely on a clean disconnect event because sockets often die without one. Up to ~30s staleness, which is fine for a status dot. > 💬 "Presence is a self-expiring Redis key — absence of a heartbeat *is* the offline signal." **6. How do connection servers find which box holds the recipient's socket?** A Redis session registry maps userId/deviceId → connection server. Fan-out workers and the message service look it up to route a push; if absent, the recipient is offline and we notify. The registry entry is TTL'd and refreshed by the same heartbeat, so it self-heals when a box dies. > 💬 "A Redis map of user-to-server tells us where to route; missing entry means offline, so we push-notify instead." **7. Why Cassandra for messages and not SQL?** The workload is append-heavy at millions of writes/sec and petabytes of data, with the only read pattern being "give me a conversation's messages in order." Cassandra's LSM-tree absorbs that write rate and partitioning by convId makes the read a single contiguous scan. SQL's b-tree writes and single-primary scaling would fall over and sharding it manually reinvents Cassandra. > 💬 "Append-heavy, ordered-by-conversation, petabyte scale — that's exactly Cassandra's sweet spot; SQL would buckle." **8. How do you do read receipts efficiently?** Each membership row carries a lastReadSeq; when a user reads up to seq N, the client sends one update and we fan that out as a lightweight event to other participants, not per-message. For large groups I aggregate (a count, not per-user receipts) to avoid N-squared receipt traffic. It's eventually consistent — receipts are not latency-critical. > 💬 "I track a single last-read sequence per member and broadcast that, not a receipt per message — and aggregate for big groups." **9. How do media attachments work?** The client requests a pre-signed upload URL, uploads directly to blob storage (S3), and the message carries only the blob reference; recipients download via CDN. The body never bloats the message DB and large transfers never touch my message path. We can scan/transcode async. > 💬 "Media goes straight to blob storage via a pre-signed URL behind a CDN; the message only carries a pointer." **10. What's the consistency model across regions?** Strong (per-conversation) within a user's home region; cross-region conversations replicate the log asynchronously, so a participant in another region may see a sub-second lag. I pin users to a home region to keep the common case fast and accept eventual cross-region convergence. A globally synchronous write would add 100ms+ per hop and kill latency. > 💬 "Strong within a region, async across regions — pinning users home keeps the hot path fast and lag is sub-second." **11. How do you prevent abuse / spam at this scale?** Rate-limit sends per user/device at the connection server using a distributed token bucket in Redis (shared state, not per-box, or a user could spread load across boxes). Layer spam-detection async on the message stream and support block/report flows. Auth on every frame via the JWT established at socket handshake. > 💬 "Distributed Redis token-bucket rate limits — shared state so you can't dodge it by hopping boxes — plus async spam scoring." **12. How would you add typing indicators / ephemeral signals?** Route them over the WebSocket as fire-and-forget frames that are NOT persisted and NOT fanned out durably — they go straight through the connection servers to online participants only. Persisting them would 10x my write load for data nobody re-reads. They're best-effort by design. > 💬 "Typing indicators ride the socket as ephemeral, unpersisted frames to online users only — never written to the DB." **13. What happens when a whole region goes down?** Health checks trip and DNS/anycast reroutes affected users to a neighboring region where their replicated message log lives; they reconnect and resume from their cursor. RPO is near-zero for cross-region-replicated conversations and RTO is minutes. In-flight, un-replicated messages are the only risk, which quorum-within-region minimizes. > 💬 "Anycast reroutes users to a healthy region with their replicated log; they reconnect on their cursor with near-zero data loss." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "I'm designing WhatsApp-scale chat: a hundred billion messages a day, half a billion concurrent connections. Clients hold WebSockets to dumb connection servers; a Message service persists each message once, stamps it with a per-conversation sequence number for ordering, and acks. Fan-out workers off a Kafka log write tiny pointers into each recipient's durable inbox — online users get pushed via a Redis session registry, offline users get an APNs/FCM doorbell and pull their backlog on reconnect using a per-device cursor. The bottleneck isn't ingesting messages, it's fan-out at ~55 million writes a second, so I go hybrid: fan-out-on-write for small groups, fan-out-on-read for megagroups. Messages live in Cassandra partitioned by conversation, media in blob storage behind a CDN, presence as a self-expiring Redis key. Once I ack, RPO is zero — every downstream failure delays delivery but never loses a message. The trade-offs I'd revisit are per-conversation versus global ordering, the fan-out threshold, and whether to keep history or delete on delivery."
